clean_story:
Flying storm hunters. Data from the eye.

The name extreme-weather drone fits like a rescue vest—built to dive where humans can’t, gathering readings from hurricanes, wildfires, or tornadoes mid-chaos.

Most weather tools watch storms from afar, like tracking a lion’s shadow instead of its claws. Drones fly into the storm. The Saildrone Surveyor, a 23-foot uncrewed boat with a wing, surfed 2021’s Hurricane Sam, streaming 150-mph wind speeds and wave heights live—data that sharpens forecasts by 10–15%. Over land, NOAA’s Coyote drone (launched from a plane) spirals into tornadoes, mapping wind shifts in 3D; its 2023 Alabama deployment cut warning times from 14 to 8 minutes.

Their edge? Disposability. A $5,000 drone lost in a hurricane costs less than risking a $60M crewed flight. Sensors pack into their frames: barometers for pressure drops, LiDAR to scan debris fields, even microphones to "hear" lightning’s frequency. One test drone in Australia’s 2019 bushfires detected ember storms—a wall of firebrands—before ground crews saw flames.

Precision matters. A 1°F error in ocean temps can misplace a hurricane’s landfall by 50 miles. Drones fix that.

Three related subjects are storm-chasing robots on wheels, AI predicting flash floods, and how animals sense disasters first.
